text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY AND BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Day +180

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: BMT Attending Physician
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient, Routine Post-Transplant Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female presenting for her routine post-allogeneic SCT day +180 follow-up visit. She is approximately 8 months from her initial AML diagnosis. She reports feeling markedly better overall compared to her last visit, with some residual skin and gastrointestinal symptoms related to chronic graft-versus-host disease that are improving.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old high school biology teacher with no prior hematologic history who was diagnosed with de novo acute myeloid leukemia (AML), NOS, at age 48. Her presenting features included a leukocyte count of approximately 87,400/uL with ~40% circulating blasts, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, platelets of 32,000/uL, and gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous bleeding. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis demonstrated 72% myeloblasts in a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity), with Auer rods present and immunophenotype consistent with AML (CD34+, CD117+, CD33+, CD13+, MPO+). Molecular workup was notable for FLT3-ITD with a high allelic ratio of 0.68, co-occurring with DNMT3A p.R882H and RUNX1 frameshift mutations, placing her in the ELN 2022 Adverse risk category. Cytogenetics showed a normal female karyotype. NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, and TP53 were all wild-type.

She received 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 100 mg/m2 continuous infusion days 1-7 and daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) with the addition of midostaurin 50 mg BID on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pulmonary infiltrates (treated with cefepime and micafungin for 14 days), grade 2 mucositis, and grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to midostaurin. Day +28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry. Given her adverse-risk disease, she proceeded expeditiously to allogeneic stem cell transplant without consolidation chemotherapy.

A 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (age 32, CMV seropositive) was identified. Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed adequate cardiac (LVEF 60%), pulmonary (FEV1 88%, DLCO 82%), and renal function. PET-CT prior to transplant demonstrated complete metabolic response with normalization of splenomegaly. She received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4, pharmacokinetically dosed) with GVHD prophylaxis consisting of tacrolimus and short-course methotrexate. Day 0 peripheral blood stem cell infusion proceeded without incident (CD34+ cell dose 5.2 x 10^6/kg). Post-transplant course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis (requiring TPN and IV morphine PCA) and a transient tacrolimus toxicity episode with creatinine rising to 1.4 mg/dL, both of which resolved. Engraftment was confirmed at day +30 with 98% donor chimerism and MRD negativity.

At her day +100 clinic visit, she developed mild-to-moderate chronic GVHD with lichenoid skin changes covering approximately 10-15% BSA on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands, along with loose stools 2-3 times daily with mild cramping. Skin punch biopsy confirmed lichenoid cGVHD (NIH grade 2). Overall NIH global cGVHD severity was scored as mild. She was started on topical clobetasol ointment for her skin and oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms, with tacrolimus taper deferred.

Today, at day +180, she returns for surveillance. She reports significant improvement in her skin GVHD -- the erythematous papules on her forearms and hands have largely resolved with ongoing topical clobetasol. She is down to applying clobetasol to small residual areas only. Her stools have improved markedly on budesonide, now 1-2 loosely formed stools per day with minimal cramping. She denies any oral mucosal pain though notes ongoing mild dryness of the mouth requiring frequent sips of water and use of a saliva substitute spray. She has no fever, no chills, no night sweats. No new skin lesions. No nausea or vomiting. No bleeding or bruising. She denies bone pain, joint pain, or muscle cramping. She reports gradual improvement in her stamina and energy levels and has begun returning to light gardening activities at home. She is not yet back to teaching but is optimistic about returning in the coming months.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue is mild and improving. No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Appetite is good; she has regained approximately 8 of the 12 pounds she lost prior to diagnosis.
Skin/Hair/Nails: As above -- improving lichenoid changes on forearms and hands. Mild generalized skin dryness. Nails intact without ridging or dystrophy.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headaches. No visual changes or ocular dryness (ophthalmology referral planned for cataract surveillance given busulfan exposure). Mild oral xerostomia as above, using saliva substitute with good effect.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea on exertion. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No cough, no shortness of breath. No wheezing.
Gastrointestinal: As above. No abdominal pain beyond mild cramping. No rectal bleeding. No hematochezia or melena.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria. Menstrual periods remain absent (amenorrheic since conditioning chemotherapy; gynecology referral planned for discussion of premature ovarian insufficiency).
Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or arthritis. No avascular necrosis symptoms.
Neurological: No focal deficits. No peripheral neuropathy symptoms. No cognitive changes.
Psychosocial: Reports some anxiety about relapse, which she is managing with mindfulness exercises. Supportive relationship with husband.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism well-controlled on levothyroxine. No new symptoms of thyroid dysfunction.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Tacrolimus (Prograf) -- trough level-guided dosing, currently approximately 1 mg PO BID (trough target 5-10 ng/mL at this stage, ongoing slow taper)
2. Letermovir (Prevymis) 480 mg PO daily (CMV prophylaxis through at least day +200)
3. Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID (HSV/VZV prophylaxis)
4. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim) 1 DS tablet PO three times weekly (PJP prophylaxis)
5. Fluconazole 100 mg PO daily (antifungal prophylaxis)
6. Budesonide (Entocort) 3 mg PO TID (GI cGVHD)
7. Clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment topically to affected skin areas BID
8. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
9. Calcium 500 mg + vitamin D3 1000 IU PO BID
10. Saliva substitute spray PRN
11. Multivitamin daily

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- AML, de novo, NOS -- as above
- Hypothyroidism -- on levothyroxine since approximately age 38
- No prior hematologic disorders, MDS, or antecedent cytopenias

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- High school biology teacher; currently on medical leave
- Non-smoker, no alcohol use, no illicit drug use
- Lives with husband (primary caregiver) in single-story home
- One sibling (7/10 HLA match, not used as donor)
- No toxic occupational exposures

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No known hematologic malignancies in first-degree relatives
- Father: type 2 diabetes
- Mother: breast cancer (postmenopausal, BRCA not tested in patient)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Pleasant affect. Appears well-nourished. Weight 62 kg (up from nadir of approximately 56 kg during transplant hospitalization; pre-illness weight 68 kg).
Vital Signs: Temperature 36.7°C, heart rate 74 bpm, blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air.
ECOG Performance Status: 0-1.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx: moist mucous membranes. Mild xerostomia. No oral ulcerations, no lichenoid changes of the oral mucosa at this time. No erythema or exudates. Sclerae non-icteric.
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normoactive bowel sounds. Spleen not palpable below the left costal margin (previously 4 cm below costal margin at diagnosis). Liver edge not appreciated.
Skin: Residual faint erythematous patches on bilateral dorsal forearms and dorsal hands, covering approximately 5% or less of total body surface area. No new papules or plaques. No vesicles. No sclerodermatous changes. No hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation in a pattern suggestive of progressive cGVHD. Nails: intact, no pterygium unguis.
Neuromuscular: Strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities. No peripheral neuropathy on light touch or vibration testing of bilateral feet. Gait normal.
Musculoskeletal: No joint effusions. No tenderness over long bones.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.9 k/uL (normal)
- ANC: 3,600/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 12.3 g/dL (improved from nadir; approaching her pre-illness baseline)
- MCV: 91 fL
- Platelets: 178,000/uL (normal)
- No circulating blasts on differential

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 140 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.85 mg/dL (resolved from tacrolimus-related elevation; back to near baseline)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normal)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT: 48 U/L (mildly elevated, trending down from prior visit)
- AST: 38 U/L (mildly elevated, trending down)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 82 U/L (normal)
- Total protein: 7.1 g/dL; Albumin: 4.0 g/dL
- Glucose: 92 mg/dL

Note: Mild transaminase elevation (ALT/AST approximately 1.5-2x ULN) is consistent with ongoing mild hepatic cGVHD involvement versus tacrolimus effect; trend is improving. LFTs will be rechecked at next visit.

Tacrolimus trough level: 8.1 ng/mL (therapeutic range maintained during slow taper)

Virology:
- CMV PCR (plasma): undetectable (on letermovir prophylaxis)
- EBV PCR (plasma): undetectable
- HHV-6: not tested at this visit

Peripheral Blood Chimerism (STR-based):
- Total chimerism: 99% donor
- CD3+ T cells: 97% donor
- CD33+ myeloid cells: ~100% donor
Comment: Full donor chimerism maintained at 6 months, reassuring against mixed chimerism or potential graft failure.

Thyroid function: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine).

Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal in the setting of tacrolimus use; oral supplementation reinforced).

---

IMAGING

No new imaging obtained at today's visit. Most recent imaging was a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained at approximately day +90, which demonstrated no evidence of extramedullary disease, no lymphadenopathy, and normal organ sizes. Mild residual ground-glass opacities in the right lower lobe noted at that time were characterized as likely representing resolving infection or engraftment syndrome and were clinically asymptomatic. Today's clinical examination and CBC are reassuring against recurrence. PET-CT for 1-year surveillance is being planned per protocol.

---

BONE MARROW BIOPSY (obtained at this visit / concurrently scheduled; see separate pathology report):
Day +180 bone marrow biopsy sent today for 6-month surveillance; results pending at time of this note but expected to show sustained remission based on clinical trajectory and chimerism data.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. AML, de novo, NOS, with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio), DNMT3A, RUNX1, and TET2 mutations -- ELN 2022 Adverse risk:
   The patient achieved morphologic CR with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin induction, followed by myeloablative allogeneic SCT (MUD, 10/10 HLA-matched, FluBu4 conditioning). She is now at day +180 post-transplant, approximately 8 months from diagnosis. CBC has fully recovered with normal counts across all cell lines. Peripheral blood chimerism shows 99% donor engraftment with 97% CD3+ T-cell donor fraction, which is excellent and argues strongly against incipient graft failure or mixed chimerism that might herald relapse. CMV and EBV PCR undetectable. The clinical picture is entirely consistent with sustained complete remission. Day +180 bone marrow biopsy and MRD assessment were sent at this visit; results will be reviewed when available and reported separately. Molecular remission assessment (FLT3-ITD PCR, DNMT3A ddPCR) sent concurrently.
   Plan: Continue current surveillance schedule. Plan PET-CT restaging at approximately 1 year post-transplant (day +365). If marrow biopsy returns in remission, the next biopsy will be deferred to 12 months post-transplant (day +365) or sooner if clinically indicated (new cytopenias, mixed chimerism, or symptoms concerning for relapse).

2. Chronic GVHD -- skin (lichenoid) and GI involvement, NIH global severity: mild:
   The patient has shown significant improvement in both skin and GI manifestations since initiation of targeted therapy at day +100. Skin: residual involvement now estimated at <5% BSA (down from 15% at peak), with active papules largely resolved on topical clobetasol. She should continue clobetasol to remaining affected areas and transition to mid-potency topical steroid (triamcinolone) as lesions continue to resolve, with goal of eventual topical steroid taper and discontinuation. Oral mucosa: no active lichenoid oral cGVHD changes at this visit; mild xerostomia managed with saliva substitutes. GI: down to 1-2 loose stools daily on budesonide 3 mg TID with mild cramping; acceptable improvement. Will continue budesonide at current dose for now and reassess at next visit with consideration of taper if symptoms continue to improve. No requirement for systemic prednisone at this time. Hepatic: mild transaminase elevation (ALT 48, AST 38) may reflect low-grade hepatic cGVHD versus tacrolimus effect; no hyperbilirubinemia or cholestatic picture. Trend is improving; no intervention beyond monitoring planned. Tacrolimus: trough 8.1 ng/mL, therapeutic. Tacrolimus taper deferred given active cGVHD. Will reassess taper timeline at day +270 follow-up visit; if skin and GI cGVHD continue to improve and remain mild/controlled, will cautiously resume taper targeting discontinuation by approximately day +365-450.

3. Prophylactic antimicrobials and infection surveillance:
   - Letermovir (CMV prophylaxis): continue through at least day +200 (approximately 3 more weeks), then reassess. Given CMV seropositivity in both donor and recipient, will transition to CMV PCR monitoring every 2 weeks after letermovir discontinuation. If CMV PCR becomes detectable above 200 IU/mL, will initiate preemptive valganciclovir therapy.
   - Acyclovir: continue for VZV/HSV prophylaxis; plan to continue for a total of at least 1 year post-transplant.
   - Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim): continue for PJP prophylaxis; planned through at least 6 months after all immunosuppression is discontinued.
   - Fluconazole: continue for antifungal prophylaxis while on tacrolimus and budesonide; will reassess at each visit.

4. Oral xerostomia:
   Likely multifactorial -- mild cGVHD effect plus residual effects of prior conditioning therapy. Continue saliva substitute spray. Reinforce good oral hygiene. Recommend regular dental check-ups (every 3-4 months while on immunosuppression). Referral to oral medicine for cGVHD-related oral care if symptoms worsen.

5. Tacrolimus-related hypomagnesemia:
   Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL. Continue oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID). Reinforce dietary sources. Repeat magnesium at next lab check.

6. Hypothyroidism:
   Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH 2.1 mIU/L. Continue current dose. Note: patients post-allogeneic SCT with busulfan-based conditioning are at increased risk for thyroid dysfunction; will monitor TFTs annually.

7. Survivorship and preventive health:
   - Premature ovarian insufficiency: patient has been amenorrheic since conditioning. Gynecology referral placed today to discuss hormonal evaluation, bone health, and fertility counseling (as applicable). Baseline FSH, LH, and estradiol ordered.
   - Bone health: DEXA scan ordered (increased osteoporosis risk from prior corticosteroid exposure, hypogonadism, and conditioning). Currently on calcium + vitamin D. Bisphosphonate therapy to be considered based on DEXA results.
   - Ocular surveillance: ophthalmology referral placed for cataract screening (busulfan-related risk) and sicca evaluation (cGVHD).
   - Cardiovascular risk: Blood pressure normal, no metabolic syndrome. Monitoring for long-term cardiovascular effects of conditioning and immunosuppression.
   - Secondary malignancy surveillance: counseled regarding elevated lifetime risk of secondary malignancies (skin cancers, post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder) following allogeneic SCT and long-term immunosuppression. Skin sun protection counseled. Annual skin check planned.
   - Vaccinations: Live vaccines remain contraindicated while on immunosuppression. Inactivated vaccines (influenza, pneumococcal, others per transplant protocol) planned according to post-transplant vaccination schedule beginning at approximately 1 year post-transplant if immunosuppression is being tapered.
   - Quality of life: Patient is making good progress in her recovery. She is engaging in light physical activity (gardening) and plans to return to teaching. Encouraged to gradually increase physical activity as tolerated. Referral to survivorship clinic placed for comprehensive care coordination.

8. Return to care:
   Follow-up in approximately 6 weeks (day +225 approximately) for interval labs, repeat LFTs, CMV PCR monitoring post-letermovir discontinuation, and clinical assessment of cGVHD. Bone marrow biopsy results and molecular MRD results from today's visit to be communicated to patient when available. Patient instructed to contact clinic urgently for any fevers above 38.0°C, new skin rash, worsening GI symptoms, bleeding, or symptoms concerning for infection.

Time spent: approximately 50 minutes (greater than 50% counseling and coordination of care).

Electronically signed by: Attending Hematologist/Bone Marrow Transplant Physician
